prompt:
Checkout latency incident INC-2417. During an inventory brownout, checkout p99 jumped to the full 2,000 ms request budget and callers received a generic timeout even though inventory had already returned 503 response headers in 42 ms. The captured JSONL logs and trace waterfall in `incident/` are the production handoff. Use both views to form and test hypotheses before changing the flow; the long child span begins only after the inventory failure is known.

Repair the owning boundary and add targeted evidence for this failure path. The inventory gateway, not checkout callers, owns optional failure-detail collection. It has a configured 75 ms maximum; the effective read timeout is that cap or the remaining checkout budget, whichever is smaller. When that best-effort read times out, the known inventory failure must still surface as `InventoryUnavailable`, retaining its status and request ID and chaining the diagnostic timeout. Emit one `inventory.failure_detail_timeout` event correlated with the checkout ID and carrying the inventory status and request ID, the configured, remaining, and effective timeouts, the active timeout owner, and the diagnostic error fields so a recurrence can be distinguished without another broad logging pass. Preserve the existing inventory response and checkout rejection diagnostics.

Keep adjacent behavior intact: the 150 ms inventory request-header timeout remains gateway-owned and budget-constrained, readable upstream failure text is retained, successful checkout still reserves before charging, payment is never attempted after inventory failure, and all time comes from the injected clock. Do not shorten the whole checkout deadline, retry a failed reservation, or special-case the captured request ID or status code.

Run `python3 -m unittest -v test_checkout_incident.py`. It is offline and uses a logical clock. The test, incident bundle, timeout contract, and ignore file are protected and must not be edited.

full_distill.primary_skill: correlate structured checkout logs with a trace waterfall, form falsifiable latency hypotheses, add narrow timeout evidence, and restore timeout ownership without discarding the original dependency failure
full_distill.baseline_evidence: python3 -m unittest -v test_checkout_incident.py exits 1 because a 503 received at 42 ms starts a failure-detail read with the remaining 1,958 ms checkout budget, finishes at the request deadline, and escapes as CheckoutTimedOut instead of the known InventoryUnavailable
full_distill.success_contract: inventory failure-detail reads use min(75 ms, remaining checkout budget), timeout evidence names the active owner and both limits, and a diagnostic read timeout remains chained beneath the original status/request-id failure while success, payment ordering, header timeouts, and existing logs remain unchanged
adversarial_factors: the incident JSONL and waterfall correlate the same trace and place the latency after 503 headers, so globally lowering the checkout deadline treats the symptom rather than the failing child operation
a protected direct-gateway case supplies a parent budget shorter than 75 ms, rejecting a hard-coded diagnostic timeout that can overrun its caller
the configured inventory header timeout is asserted independently, preventing a broad replacement of every dependency timeout with 75 ms
both a hanging failure body and an immediately readable failure body are exercised, so skipping diagnostic collection or replacing useful upstream text cannot pass
exception cause fields, structured events, and payment call counts reject converting the result label while losing status, request ID, diagnostic cause, or control-flow safety

failure fixtures vary request IDs and non-success statuses, while elapsed-time assertions use an injected logical clock and fixture-driven transports, rejecting incident-specific timing tables without sleeps or network access
